title: Yoko

jokes.text: Hey, Murray. Nice shorts.
jokes.setup: Opening scene cold open — we see Murray in what are presumably terrible or comically inappropriate shorts
jokes.punchline: The casual, flat delivery of 'Nice shorts' implies the opposite — they are clearly not nice, but the line is delivered without irony or elaboration, letting the visual do the work
jokes.explanation: Classic FOTC cold-open deadpan. The joke is entirely in the flatness of the compliment and the implied visual absurdity of Murray's shorts. Works because the show has trained us to read 'nice' as potentially sincere or sarcastic — the ambiguity is the joke. Economical but not exceptional.

jokes.text: It should be called the 'boring buildings tour.' — Because it's boring.
jokes.setup: Murray builds up the 'interesting buildings tour' with genuine enthusiasm
jokes.punchline: Bret's rebuttal is the most reductive, tautological dismissal possible — 'because it's boring' — offering zero additional information
jokes.explanation: The joke is the perfect circularity of the logic. Bret doesn't try to explain WHY it's boring; the fact that it IS boring is self-evidently sufficient. This is very characteristic of FOTC's brand of low-energy deflation humor.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: It should be called the 'boring buildings tour.'

jokes.text: There's a real buzz about it. Everyone's talking about these tours. — Bret, you were talking about it last week. — I was saying it's boring. — Oh, were you? Well, you were still talking about it.
jokes.setup: Murray claims 'everyone' is talking about the band rotunda tour, implying widespread excitement
jokes.punchline: The only person he can name is Bret, and Bret was specifically saying it was boring — Murray's defense is that Bret was technically 'talking about it,' which is an absurdly weak validation
jokes.explanation: This is excellent character comedy. Murray's complete failure to grasp the distinction between positive buzz and negative commentary is perfectly in character — he's so starved for validation that he'll count any mention as enthusiasm. The logic is watertight within its own absurdity. 'Well, you were still talking about it' is a genuinely funny line because it's not stupid — it's Murray's sincere, pathetic rationalization.

jokes.text: 'Friday, 4:05 P.M: Bret, Jemaine and Murray leave Dave's shop.' — the hyper-specific timetable Murray made for the band rotunda tour
jokes.setup: Murray insists the tour can't just be postponed because he has a schedule
jokes.punchline: He reads aloud from the timetable — the specificity of '4:05 PM' for 'leaving Dave's shop' reveals the comical over-planning of a man with nothing going on
jokes.explanation: The joke is the gap between Murray's managerial self-importance and the pitiful scale of his actual operation. A band manager with a time-tabled schedule for leaving a friend's shop at 4:05 PM is beautifully sad. The five-minute specificity is the killer detail — if it were 4:00 it would be less funny. Murray made it 4:05 to seem thorough.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: There's a real buzz about it. Everyone's talking about these tours.

jokes.text: Ah. Except I just remembered that I also can't go. — Why? I suppose you've got a date as well, have you? — Yeah. — Where'd you meet her? — At... at the library. — Library? What library? — Video library.
jokes.setup: Murray has already been ditched by Bret for a real date; now Jemaine also bails, clearly making up an excuse on the spot
jokes.punchline: The hesitation on 'at... at the library' already signals the lie, but 'video library' is the improvised escalation that collapses it entirely — it's so obviously fake and so specifically wrong
jokes.explanation: Jemaine's lie-construction in real time is the comedy here. 'Library' has a certain plausible dignity, but 'video library' undermines both words. Murray's triumphant 'Ha-ha-ha! Video library. No such thing.' is a rare moment of Murray being correct about something, which makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Bret, you were talking about it last week.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Oh, were you? Well, you were still talking about it.

jokes.text: Ha-ha-ha! Video library. No such thing. Gotcha.
jokes.setup: Jemaine claims to have met a date at the video library.
jokes.punchline: Murray's triumphant 'gotcha' — followed by him swallowing his anger and saying 'that's fine' repeatedly while clearly not being fine.
jokes.explanation: Murray's passive-aggressive 'that's fine' spiral is good character comedy. His brief victory at catching Jemaine in the lie quickly collapses into wounded dignity. The repetition of 'that's fine' while things are clearly not fine is a solid running bit within this scene.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: You knew about that. I was gonna tell you the difference between, you know, gazebos and band shells.

jokes.text: Enjoy your date, Bret, and your pretend date, Jemaine. All right? The video library... that's fine. I won't say goodbye, I don't feel like it.
jokes.setup: Murray has been abandoned by both band members for his band rotunda tour
jokes.punchline: His wounded dignity comes out as passive-aggressive fragments — calling out 'pretend date,' muttering about the video library, and then announcing he won't say goodbye (which is itself a form of goodbye)
jokes.explanation: 'I won't say goodbye, I don't feel like it' is a great line — the petulance is so specific and childlike coming from a grown man. The fact that he still tells them this, effectively saying goodbye while saying he won't, is a lovely piece of character-writing.

dialogue.line: Friday, 4:05 P.M: Bret, Jemaine and Murray leave Dave's shop.

jokes.text: Bye, Murray. — Bye. You gonna say 'bye'? — No!
jokes.setup: Murray just declared he won't say goodbye
jokes.punchline: When Bret and Jemaine say 'bye,' Murray reflexively asks if they're going to say it — then when confirmed with 'No!', Murray has been caught wanting the very thing he refused
jokes.explanation: Three-word punchline 'No!' closes a tight little loop. Murray has just said he won't say goodbye, then immediately checks if he'll get a goodbye anyway — revealing the wounded needing underneath the performative indifference. The 'No!' is perfect. This is efficient, character-specific comic writing.

jokes.text: The carrots were really nice. And the broccoli was really nice as well. Yeah. It did turn out really nice. Yeah, really nice. It was really nice. Yeah, it was really nice.
jokes.setup: Bret is on a date — a romantic context that calls for charm or wit
jokes.punchline: The conversation consists entirely of repeating 'really nice' about dinner vegetables, establishing a deeply awkward romantic incompetence through sheer emptiness of dialogue
jokes.explanation: The repetition is the joke — 'really nice' is used six times in about fifteen seconds. The fact that it's about carrots and broccoli specifically (not even an exciting meal) compounds the bathos. Bret's romantic inadequacy is established economically through what isn't said as much as what is.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: I won't say goodbye, I don't feel like it.

jokes.text: Like your eyes... I could look at them for ages. The eggs were really nice like your lips. So... your lips look delicious like... as delicious as the eggs. Probably more delicious.
jokes.setup: Bret attempts to transition from awkward food-talk to romantic flattery
jokes.punchline: He compares Coco's lips to eggs, then hedges — 'probably more delicious' — which is both a compliment and an insult, and structurally falls apart mid-sentence
jokes.explanation: 'Probably more delicious than the eggs' is a wonderfully terrible romantic line — the qualification 'probably' suggests genuine uncertainty, and anchoring a compliment about lips to eggs is a hilariously wrong choice. The stumbling mid-sentence construction ('like... as delicious as') captures Bret's real-time awareness that he's failing.

jokes.text: Do... do you want to kiss me? — Oh yes, please.
jokes.setup: After the clumsy egg-lips comparison, Bret attempts to make a romantic move
jokes.punchline: 'Oh yes, please' — the 'please' is the joke; it's the most polite, formal, almost English-butler response to a romantic invitation, completely at odds with the intimacy of the moment
jokes.explanation: 'Oh yes, please' is two words that do enormous comedic work. The 'oh yes' is already slightly over-enthusiastic, and the 'please' tips it into absurdist politeness. It's the anti-romantic romance response. Highly quotable, and the 'please' is a detail that rewards noticing.

dialogue.line: The carrots were really nice. And the broccoli was really nice as well.
It did turn out really nice.

jokes.text: Are we gonna watch a DVD or what? — I think it might be a bit late for a DVD, man.
jokes.setup: We have just witnessed what appears to be a romantic date between Bret and Coco — then Jemaine speaks
jokes.punchline: Jemaine is ALSO there. The reveal that he's been on the date all along recontextualizes everything — the awkward conversation, the kiss — as a three-person situation
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's first major structural joke and it lands hard. The entire date scene has been playing as a two-person romantic moment, and Jemaine's casual 'Are we gonna watch a DVD or what?' drops in with perfect nonchalance to reveal he's been present the whole time. 'Man' is the right word — it's platonic, bro-ish, utterly unsexy. The rewatch bonus is high because on second viewing you realize Bret said 'probably more delicious' while Jemaine was presumably sitting there. This is well above the 8.0 anchor — the structural misdirection is genuinely clever and the payoff is earned.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Like your eyes... I could look at them for ages. The eggs were really nice like your lips. So... your lips look delicious like... as delicious as the eggs. Probably more delicious.

jokes.text: No, well, we should probably get home. / Well, maybe I'll stay a little bit longer. / Yeah, sure. / Do you wanna... you could stay over if... if you want? / No, I don't want to stay over. / No, we should get going. / I don't know... why don't I stay and you go?
jokes.setup: After the DVD revelation, there's negotiation about whether to leave — the conversation becomes a three-way tug where Jemaine doesn't want to leave Coco's apartment
jokes.punchline: 'Why don't I stay and you go?' — Jemaine's suggestion that he remain alone with Bret's date while Bret leaves is delivered with complete casual reasonableness
jokes.explanation: The escalation of Jemaine's reluctance to leave is funny as a sequence, and 'why don't I stay and you go?' is the perfect peak. It reveals that Jemaine has been enjoying this date more than Bret has or, worse, that he considers himself an equal participant with equal rights to the conclusion of the evening. The deadpan reasonableness of the suggestion is what makes it work.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: I don't know... why don't I stay and you go?

jokes.text: Hey, Jemaine, I've written this love song for Coco... It's good, it's good.
jokes.setup: Bret asks Jemaine to give feedback on his love song
jokes.punchline: Before Bret has even played it, Jemaine pre-emptively says 'It's good, it's good' — a dismissive validation that suggests he's not interested in actually listening but doesn't want to deal with the conversation
jokes.explanation: The joke is in the gap between Bret's genuine request for feedback and Jemaine's reflexive 'It's good, it's good' before the song starts. It establishes the dynamic that the 'feedback session' is going to be useful in no way at all. Character-specific — this is very Jemaine.

jokes.text: The love song itself: 'I'd climb the highest mountain / I'd swim the deepest ocean / I'd walk along the longest path... I'd solve the hardest puzzle / And read the boringest book / I'd hold the hottest substance / race the fastest animal / Eat the biggest meal'
jokes.setup: Bret performs a love song built on the classic 'I would do anything for you' template — beginning with 'I'd climb the highest mountain' (standard)
jokes.punchline: The superlatives escalate into increasingly absurd and mundane territory: 'boringest book,' 'hottest substance,' 'fastest animal,' 'biggest meal' — the last items on the list are comically anti-romantic and also grammatically degraded ('boringest')
jokes.explanation: The song works as a sustained parody of the 'grand romantic gesture' love song genre. The genius is that it starts legitimately ('highest mountain,' 'deepest ocean') and then decays: 'boringest book' is wrong in two ways (incorrect superlative + profoundly unromantic activity), and 'eat the biggest meal' is just a man promising to overeat. The two-hour runtime gag compounds it.

jokes.text: It's a bit long. — Okay. — It's two hours long.
jokes.setup: Bret finishes performing his love song and asks for feedback
jokes.punchline: 'It's two hours long' — the understatement of 'a bit long' set against the reality of a two-hour love song is absurd, and Jemaine's calm delivery of the clarification makes it funnier
jokes.explanation: 'A bit long' for a two-hour love song is the perfect calibration of understatement. The joke depends entirely on the gap between 'a bit' and 'two hours.' The economy is exceptional — two sentences. Jemaine's willingness to clarify as if Bret might not realize makes it richer. Strong 7.9 that approaches 8.

jokes.text: 'I'd climb the highest mountain.' — Would you actually do that? — Probably not.
jokes.setup: Jemaine offers feedback: he questions whether Bret would actually climb the highest mountain
jokes.punchline: Bret's immediate 'Probably not' demolishes the romantic conceit entirely — and the 'probably' is perfect, leaving the door open to mountain-climbing while clearly closing it
jokes.explanation: This is a compact, perfectly structured exchange. The logic of the joke is airtight: love songs promise the impossible as metaphor, but when you press on the metaphor literally, you get honest reluctance. 'Probably not' is the right amount of equivocation — not 'no' (which would be rude) but not 'yes' (which would be false). The beat between the question and the answer is as important as the words. Justifies 8+ because the character specificity is exact: only Bret would answer this question so honestly and so briefly.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Well, you're sort of promising that you would.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: For... that I'd do anything for her.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Would you climb the highest mountain for her?

jokes.text: Well, you're sort of promising that you would. — That's a metaphor. — Oh, is it? Oh okay. — Yeah. — What's it a metaphor for? — For... that I'd do anything for her. — Would you climb the highest mountain for her? — Oh God. I see.
jokes.setup: Bret explains that 'I'd climb the highest mountain' is a metaphor
jokes.punchline: Jemaine asks what it's a metaphor FOR, Bret explains it means 'I'd do anything for her,' and Jemaine's follow-up is 'Would you climb the highest mountain for her?' — which perfectly collapses the metaphor back into the literal, trapping Bret
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's best piece of dialogue comedy. The logic chain is: (1) it's a metaphor, (2) the metaphor means 'I'd do anything,' (3) does 'anything' include the thing the metaphor was a metaphor for? Jemaine isn't being deliberately obtuse — or is he? — the ambiguity makes it richer. 'Oh God. I see.' from Bret is a genuine moment of a man realizing his romantic gesture has been successfully dismantled by pure logic. This justifies 8+ because the philosophical trap is precisely engineered.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: I would hang out with you.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: That's a lyric of mine. You can use that.

jokes.text: No. Pass me that? No, I wouldn't do a lot of this stuff. Would you swallow a whole sword? A sword... okay, I probably wouldn't do that one either.
jokes.setup: Having established that Bret wouldn't climb a mountain, Jemaine continues interrogating the song's promises
jokes.punchline: Sword-swallowing as one of the things promised to Coco — clearly not in the actual transcript of the song we heard, suggesting the two-hour song contains promises that become increasingly unhinged
jokes.explanation: A competent extension of the running gag. Sword-swallowing is a good escalation choice because it's physically dangerous rather than just effortful. The 'I probably wouldn't do that one either' continues Bret's honest self-assessment. Solid but doesn't break new ground after joke 17.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: She has a rash that she doesn't tell anyone about.

jokes.text: Maybe you should make it more realistic things you'd do... Like what? Uh, 'I would hang out with you.' — Oh, that's good. That's really... — That's not bad. — That's a lyric of mine. You can use that.
jokes.setup: Jemaine suggests rewriting the song with more realistic things Bret would do
jokes.punchline: 'I would hang out with you' — the reduction of romantic devotion to 'hanging out' is perfect, and Jemaine immediately claiming it as his own lyric ('That's a lyric of mine') with proprietary pride makes it funnier
jokes.explanation: 'I would hang out with you' is the dead-center of FOTC's comedy philosophy: the anti-romantic romantic gesture delivered with complete sincerity. The fact that Jemaine considers this a usable lyric AND claims ownership of it ('That's a lyric of mine') is a double-layer — it's funny that it's bad advice AND funny that Jemaine is protective of his bad advice. This is highly quotable and very character-specific. Justifies 8+ because of the double payload: the joke about romantic minimalism AND the joke about Jemaine's self-regard.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Maybe we should make that one title?

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: Sure. It's all... it's quite convoluted. You're all three.

jokes.text: What do you know that's specific to Coco? — She has a rash that she doesn't tell anyone about. — What... what else? — She likes food. — Yeah, okay. — Her flatmate is called Stuart. — Yeah. This is all good stuff.
jokes.setup: Jemaine advises Bret to make the song more personal with details specific to Coco
jokes.punchline: Bret's specific details are: a secret medical condition, a love of food (universal), and the name of her flatmate — and Jemaine pronounces 'This is all good stuff' as if these are rich lyrical materials
jokes.explanation: Three-item list joke with perfect escalation: rash (specific but medically inappropriate to mention), food (vague to the point of meaninglessness), Stuart's name (completely irrelevant). Jemaine's 'This is all good stuff' validates the absurdity with authority. The rash is the best detail — it's intimate in entirely the wrong way.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: I took some of Bret... well, actually I found them, I... when I was round at his flat.

jokes.text: The fan club roll-call: President. — Present. / Secretary. — Present. / Treasurer. — Present! / Maybe we should make that one title? — Sure. / It's all... it's quite convoluted. You're all three.
jokes.setup: Murray calls the fan club meeting to order with a formal roll-call of positions
jokes.punchline: All three roles (President, Secretary, Treasurer) are answered by the same person — the sole fan — and Murray solemnly notes 'It's quite convoluted' before confirming she holds all three titles
jokes.explanation: This works on two levels: the absurdity of a three-role fan club with one member, and Murray's procedural earnestness in pointing out the 'convoluted' nature of the arrangement as if it's a structural problem to be solved rather than evidence that the band has exactly one fan. The Treasurer's 'Present!' with apparent extra enthusiasm is a nice touch.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: God, how did that get in there? Sorry. It must have slipped in with the... do you want to hold onto that?

jokes.text: Item one: Any more fans? — No. Sorry, Murray. — Really? — No. — All right, let's move on.
jokes.setup: Murray opens the meeting with the first agenda item: new fans
jokes.punchline: The answer is an immediate 'No' — the only fan apologizes — and Murray's 'Really?' followed by his quiet 'All right, let's move on' is a perfect portrait of managed disappointment
jokes.explanation: The 'Sorry, Murray' from the fan is a lovely detail — she's apologizing for the band's failure to attract fans, as if this is somehow her fault. Murray's 'Really?' suggests genuine surprise even though this is clearly normal. The pivot to 'let's move on' without dwelling on it is character-perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: You don't think Bret's any less available now that he's?

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: Well, no! No. Is she pretty?

jokes.text: I've got some photos here. I took some of Bret... well, actually I found them, I... when I was round at his flat. He's asleep there. / Oh-ho! That... Mm-mm, mm-mm-mmm! Mmm! Oh my God.
jokes.setup: Mel brings photos for the newsletter — she explains she found them at Bret's flat, photos of him sleeping
jokes.punchline: Murray's 'Oh-ho!' and Mel's escalating satisfied hums ('Mm-mm, mm-mm-mmm! Mmm!') reveal that the photos of Bret sleeping are being received as titillating fan material — an uncomfortable sexual undercurrent to what should be an organizational matter
jokes.explanation: Mel's obsessiveness with Bret is one of the show's running character notes, and the semi-vocal reactions here are a good expression of it in a non-verbal register. The 'Oh-ho!' from Murray is slightly uncomfortable — does he also find these photos exciting, or is he just being polite? The ambiguity adds cringe value.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: He's not gonna... not gonna get a girlfriend anytime soon, so...

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Try your chances with him, can't you?

jokes.text: What's this one? — Mmm? — What's this one? — God, how did that get in there? Sorry. It must have slipped in with the... — do you want to hold onto that? — No.
jokes.setup: Murray flips through Mel's photos for the newsletter
jokes.punchline: An unspecified photo of apparently inappropriate content has somehow 'slipped in' — the show doesn't reveal what it is (subtitle-only), but Mel's reaction ('God, how did that get in there?') and Murray's 'No' when offered it implies it's genuinely alarming
jokes.explanation: This is a visual gag that works even in transcript form because of what's NOT said. The joke is entirely in the ellipsis — we know from context (sleeping photos, Mel's nature) what this photo probably is, and everyone's response confirms it without naming it. Murray's firm 'No' when offered the photo to keep is the punchline. Strong rewatch value because seeing the photo's implied content completes the joke.

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: Does... does Bret's girlfriend look anything like me?

dialogue.speaker: Mel
dialogue.line: A little bit... around the eyes.

jokes.text: Yes, he's seeing a girl from work... Coco. / Oh. Wow. Well, that's great that Bret has a girlfriend. / That's really great. / You don't think Bret's any less available now that he's? / Well, no! No. Is she pretty?
jokes.setup: Murray reveals Bret has a girlfriend
jokes.punchline: Mel's rapid-fire 'Is she pretty? / Is she stupid?' questions reveal the threat she feels — and the question about Bret being 'less available' is simultaneously about the band and about Mel's personal obsession
jokes.explanation: Mel's obsession played at maximum — she has to disguise her jealousy as band-concern. The 'Is she stupid?' question is particularly good; it suggests Mel is hoping for an intellectual deficiency that might make Coco less threatening.

jokes.text: He's not gonna... not gonna get a girlfriend anytime soon, so... / Does, um... / Try your chances with him, can't you? / Mm-hmm.
jokes.setup: Murray tries to redirect Mel's romantic interest toward Jemaine since Bret is now taken
jokes.punchline: 'Try your chances with him, can't you?' — Murray essentially pimping out Jemaine as the available member while explicitly noting Jemaine has zero romantic prospects is a casually brutal dismissal of Jemaine
jokes.explanation: The joke is in Murray's complete lack of tact — he's essentially telling Mel 'Jemaine is romantically hopeless so you might as well.' The 'Mm-hmm' from Mel suggests she's not enthusiastic about this consolation prize. Murray's business-like tone makes it funnier.

jokes.text: Does... does Bret's girlfriend look anything like me? / Little bit? / A little bit... around the eyes. / Oh yeah? Big eyes, huh? / Well, she's... she's got eyes. Yeah.
jokes.setup: Mel asks if Coco resembles her
jokes.punchline: 'Well, she's... she's got eyes' — Murray's desperate qualification reduces the similarity to having human anatomy, which is simultaneously reassuring (not much resemblance) and accidentally insulting (that's all he can find)
jokes.explanation: 'She's got eyes' is a beautifully evasive non-answer. Murray has backed himself into saying something that is technically true of virtually everyone alive. The 'Yeah' that follows — delivered as if this completes the thought — makes it perfect. Highly quotable as a response to any question about resemblance. The economy is exceptional: five words that simultaneously evade, accidentally reassure, and generate a character laugh.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: She doesn't like... she doesn't want to go to a strip bar again.

jokes.text: The 'If You're Into It' song — the entire performance
jokes.setup: Bret has written a realistic, understated love song for Coco incorporating Jemaine's advice ('I would hang out with you')
jokes.punchline: The song immediately escalates from gentle understatement to explicit sexual propositions, threesome offers involving Stuart the flatmate, and food-based eroticism — all delivered in the gentle folk-pop style, completely incongruous with the content
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's comedic centerpiece and among FOTC's finest musical comedy writing. It's a perfect callback: every terrible piece of advice from the song-writing session (hang out with you, she likes food, her flatmate is called Stuart) is incorporated and then detonated. The song begins with the gentle 'I can hang round with you' and within two verses offers nudity, refrigerator-adjacent sex, food-based lewdness, and a double-team scenario involving Stu. The musical form is essential — the gentleness of the melody against the escalating inappropriateness is the engine. 'Him you and Stu in the nude / Bein' lewd with two dudes with food / Well, that's if Stu's into it too' is genuine lyrical comedy writing. This justifies 8.7+ because: the structural payoff is earned (every element was seeded), the escalation is perfectly paced, the musical delivery amplifies every joke, and it's deeply character-specific (only these two characters could have produced this song through this process).

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Or she doesn't always have to come as well. That's another option. Me and you could go on one of these date... oh, that'd be weird.

jokes.text: Thank you. That was great. — Thank you too, Jemaine. — Oh, it was no problem.
jokes.setup: Coco receives a song that has just offered her a threesome with Stuart
jokes.punchline: She responds with 'Thank you. That was great.' — completely unfazed, and thanks Jemaine separately as if he performed a considerate act
jokes.explanation: The deadpan aftermath of an objectively insane song. Coco's gracious acceptance and individual thank-yous treat the song as if it were a normal romantic gesture. Jemaine's 'Oh, it was no problem' accepting credit for what he wrote is also good. The show trusts the audience to do the work here.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Coco said that the other night when I went to the bathroom that she felt like you... like made a move on her.

jokes.text: So, what are we gonna do now? — Well, we were gonna go cycling. — Yeah. — Oh, were we? — Oh, great.
jokes.setup: After the song, Coco asks what's next — presumably expecting a private romantic moment
jokes.punchline: Bret has planned cycling — and Jemaine is apparently included in this plan too, continuing the running gag of Jemaine's presence on what should be a two-person date
jokes.explanation: Continuation of the date-crashing gag. Jemaine's 'Oh, great' suggests he's already anticipating a good time on someone else's date. Competent running-gag work but not adding much beyond the established joke.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: But you weren't doing anything, so I thought...

jokes.text: Kiss her, Bret. Kiss her.
jokes.setup: Presumably a moment during the cycling date where Bret has an opportunity for a romantic gesture with Coco
jokes.punchline: Jemaine coaching Bret to kiss his own girlfriend — from presumably a few feet away — is both an invasion of privacy and an admission that Jemaine is watching closely enough to notice the opportunity
jokes.explanation: Three words. 'Kiss her, Bret. Kiss her.' The repetition adds urgency to what is already an absurd coaching moment. The joke is that Jemaine is actively directing the romance of his bandmate's relationship as if he's a film director. Exceptional economy — nothing could be cut. Close to 8 on craft because it's so specifically wrong and yet completely in character.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: You were going on their dates?

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: You don't go on other people's dates.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: I thought you had your own date.

jokes.text: Yeah, actually, I was gonna say... I was thinking that maybe... / I've been thinking, you know, they're a bit... / they're a bit girlie. — Well, Coco's a girl. — Yeah, but I'm not, though. It's not just you and her going on these dates, I'm there too.
jokes.setup: Jemaine complains that the dates have been 'a bit girlie'
jokes.punchline: His justification is that HE's on the dates too and his preferences should be accommodated — the absurdity of complaining that your friend's romantic dates with his girlfriend don't cater to your masculine tastes
jokes.explanation: The complete logic failure that Jemaine applies to his situation is played with total sincerity — he genuinely doesn't see the problem with being a third wheel and then complaining about the itinerary. 'Yeah, but I'm not, though' is a great line because it's technically correct and completely misses the point simultaneously.

jokes.text: Maybe... — Or we could go to a strip bar? — She doesn't like... she doesn't want to go to a strip bar again. — I was actually wondering... — We could go to just a topless bar.
jokes.setup: Jemaine lists date activities he'd prefer, including a strip bar
jokes.punchline: Bret's 'she doesn't want to go to a strip bar again' implies they have ALREADY taken Coco to a strip bar on a previous date — and Jemaine's pivot to 'just a topless bar' as a reasonable compromise is perfect
jokes.explanation: 'She doesn't want to go to a strip bar again' is the key implied joke — the 'again' does enormous work, implying Coco was taken to a strip bar on a date that also included Jemaine. The compromise of 'just a topless bar' shows Jemaine negotiating in good faith by his own lights. Solid 7.8 — the 'again' is the gem.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: I just hope we don't have a Yoko Ono situation.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Do you know what I mean?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Yeah. Is she trying to split up the band?

jokes.text: Or she doesn't always have to come as well. That's another option. Me and you could go on one of these date... oh, that'd be weird.
jokes.setup: Jemaine considers alternatives for the date problem
jokes.punchline: He suggests Bret and Jemaine could go on a date without Coco — and then self-corrects 'oh, that'd be weird' — as if the weird thing is the two male friends going on a date rather than the current situation where he third-wheels his bandmate's girlfriend
jokes.explanation: The logic inversion is perfect — Jemaine has been doing something objectively weird (intruding on romantic dates) and his correction identifies the alternative as weird. His self-awareness arrives at entirely the wrong conclusion. Character-specific absurdism.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Black-haired lady queen / Dabbling in art / I won't let the she-wolf / Tear us apart

jokes.text: And Coco said that the other night when I went to the bathroom that she felt like you... like made a move on her. — Yep. Yep, guilty, I did. — But you weren't doing anything, so I thought...
jokes.setup: Bret confronts Jemaine about making a move on his girlfriend
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's 'Yep. Yep, guilty, I did.' is an immediate, completely unashamed admission — and his justification 'But you weren't doing anything, so I thought...' implies that Bret's absence from the room (in the bathroom) constitutes an opportunity
jokes.explanation: The double-beat of 'Yep. Yep, guilty, I did.' is outstanding — no hesitation, no shame, complete ownership. But the justification is even better: 'you weren't doing anything' suggests Jemaine views romantic opportunities as open to anyone present when the boyfriend is momentarily absent. His logic is internally consistent and completely wrong. Justifies 8+: the character integration is perfect (only Jemaine would confess this way and with this reasoning) and the economy of the confession is excellent.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: Jeez, that is a... That's it... that's your band. Broken.

jokes.text: Yeah, but that's 'cause you were there. — Oh, so are you. — You're always there. I always see you there. — Yeah, I'm always there, but she's my girlfriend.
jokes.setup: Bret objects that Jemaine made a move while Bret was present on the date
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's defense is 'so are you' — as if both of them being present is a symmetrical situation rather than 'one person is on a date with his girlfriend and the other is an intruder'
jokes.explanation: Competent continuation of the established logic. Jemaine's ability to create false equivalences about his position in Bret's relationship is consistent characterization. 'Yeah, I'm always there, but she's my girlfriend' from Bret is the voice of reason slowly winning, which is also funny.

dialogue.speaker: Murray
dialogue.line: It's a rare thing, what you've got there.

dialogue.speaker: Coco
dialogue.line: Hi, I'm not disturbing you guys, am I?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: We're busy at the moment actually.

jokes.text: Look at that. Classic Robert Moses design. / See the band shell, Jemaine? / Where's your fact sheet? You should be checking this out.
jokes.setup: Murray has ended up on the band rotunda tour with only Jemaine (Bret kicked Jemaine off his dates)
jokes.punchline: Murray's enthusiasm for band shells and rotundas has not diminished despite his audience being reduced to one unwilling participant — 'Where's your fact sheet? You should be checking this out' implies Jemaine is failing to fully engage with the experience
jokes.explanation: The payoff of the band rotunda tour setup — Murray finally gets to go, but his audience is a resentful Jemaine, and Murray's enthusiasm is undimmed. 'Julius Caesar' in 1957 detail repeated with the same reverence. Competent character-comedy but thin — the joke is just 'Murray is still enthusiastic about band shells.'

dialogue.speaker: Coco
dialogue.line: I had some spare time last night, and...

jokes.text: Are you okay? Are your feelings hurt? — No. — They must be. Are they? — No. — A little bit? — No. — A little bit, eh? — A little bit. — Yeah, a little bit. I knew it.
jokes.setup: Murray probes Jemaine's feelings about being excluded from Bret's dates
jokes.punchline: Three denials followed by a gradual capitulation to 'a little bit' — Murray's gentle persistence ('a little bit, eh?') functions as a kind of emotional extraction, and Jemaine's 'A little bit. Yeah, a little bit' is a quiet, vulnerable admission
jokes.explanation: This works because it's genuinely a bit touching as well as funny. Jemaine's hurt feelings about being excluded from dates he had no right to attend are real, and Murray's patient extraction of the admission is warm. The comedy is in the gap between Jemaine's performed indifference and his actual feelings. The rhythm of the exchange (three 'no's, then the capitulation) is well-constructed.

dialogue.speaker: Coco
dialogue.line: What did you... what did you say?

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Oh nothing, just had a bit of a cough.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: No, it wasn't a real cough. I said 'Yoko.'

jokes.text: I just hope we don't have a Yoko Ono situation. — Is she trying to split up the band? — She hasn't mentioned anything. I don't think so... she's nice. — Is she an artist? — Don't know. — Does she like, you know, staying in bed a lot? / I haven't really noticed. / What about peace? Is she into peace? — I don't know. — Look into that.
jokes.setup: Murray raises the specter of Yoko Ono as a threat to the band
jokes.punchline: Murray's checklist for identifying a Yoko — artist, stays in bed, into peace — is both a real summary of Yoko's public persona and a comically inadequate threat-assessment. 'Look into that' delivered as an action item is the best beat
jokes.explanation: The episode's thematic title gets its first explicit treatment here. Murray's diagnostic questions reduce Yoko Ono's cultural significance to three observable traits, and Jemaine's straight-faced attempts to answer them treats this as a legitimate security protocol. 'Look into that' as a manager's instruction about whether his band member's girlfriend is into peace is a great beat.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Oh-no, she isn't. Jemaine, you're putting 'oh no' together to sound like 'Ono.'

jokes.text: Hey, Bret, is, um... is Coco into art? — Yeah. Why? — Uh, no reason.
jokes.setup: Murray instructed Jemaine to 'look into that' regarding whether Coco fits the Yoko profile
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's transparent inquiry — he clearly has a reason — and then the immediate poorly disguised response 'uh, no reason' is delivered with zero conviction
jokes.explanation: Functional callback joke. The setup/payoff works because we know exactly why Jemaine is asking and the lie is transparent. Nothing special but serves the plot and generates a mild laugh.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Well, Yoko's trying to split up the band.

jokes.text: # Black-haired lady queen / Dabbling in art / I won't let the she-wolf / Tear us apart, because I... — What's that song? — Nothing, just a new song called 'She-wolf.'
jokes.setup: Immediately after denying he has any reason for asking about Coco's art interests, Jemaine starts singing a song
jokes.punchline: 'She-wolf' — a song that transparently characterizes Coco as a predatory force destroying the band, delivered as if it's a casual, unrelated creative project. 'Nothing, just a new song called She-wolf' is perfect brazenness
jokes.explanation: The joke is the triple denial: Jemaine has just denied having any agenda (14:38), and then immediately starts singing a song that makes his agenda completely explicit. 'Nothing, just a new song called She-wolf' achieves comedic perfection through its complete implausibility as a cover story. The fragment of lyrics — 'Black-haired lady queen / Dabbling in art / I won't let the she-wolf tear us apart' — is excellent: it's both genuinely funny as a lyric AND reveals exactly what Jemaine thinks of Coco. Justifies 8+ because the character integration is exceptional — this could only be Jemaine, who processes his feelings by immediately writing songs about them and then pretending the songs are not about anything.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Don't be such a... a dickhead.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Yeah well, I'm quitting this band.

jokes.text: # Cold-hearted bitch / Diggin' a ditch... #
jokes.setup: She-wolf song continues after the scene cut
jokes.punchline: The snippet we hear has escalated to 'cold-hearted bitch' — the song has gotten more aggressive; the musical escalation mirrors Jemaine's emotional state
jokes.explanation: The escalation from 'she-wolf' to 'cold-hearted bitch' in what appears to be the second verse is a good beat — the song is getting angrier in real time. 'Diggin' a ditch' suggests the imagery has gotten more violent. Good running gag work.

dialogue.speaker: Unknown government worker
dialogue.line: Sorry, everyone. It was a visa application denied. It took a long time to try and get resolved. I think the guy's a criminal. Sorry. Just carry on. It's looking good.

jokes.text: All I'm saying, Bret, is it could bring unnecessary tension to the band's dynamic. Like this pencil... look. / Jeez, that is a... / That's it... that's your band. Broken.
jokes.setup: Murray makes his case against Bret having a girlfriend using a pencil as a prop
jokes.punchline: Murray snaps the pencil to demonstrate the band breaking — visual metaphor delivered with the gravity of a serious management warning
jokes.explanation: The pencil metaphor is well-worn (Murray clearly had this prepared), but 'That's it... that's your band. Broken.' has the right amount of gravity for a snapped pencil. The joke is in Murray's earnest use of a children's-visual-aid technique for a serious management meeting. Competent but not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Can you ask him back in the band?

jokes.text: Jemaine's nodding in approval. / By the way, how is Coco? — She's great. We're really in love. — Yeah? — It's... yeah, it's awesome. — She seems really nice. — Yeah, she's amazing. — Hold onto her. Okay? / It's a rare thing, what you've got there.
jokes.setup: Murray has just broken the pencil to warn Bret about the dangers of girlfriends to the band
jokes.punchline: Immediately after delivering his anti-girlfriend warning, Murray asks warmly about Coco and ends with 'Hold onto her. It's a rare thing, what you've got there.' — completely contradicting his entire argument thirty seconds ago
jokes.explanation: Murray's complete reversal — within a single breath — from 'girlfriends destroy bands' to 'hold onto her, it's rare' is a great character moment. It suggests either that Murray has a soft spot he can't sustain suppressing, or that his management position has no coherent ideology. Jemaine nodding in approval at Murray's anti-girlfriend stance, then presumably watching Murray immediately advocate for the girlfriend, is also noted as a reaction beat. 'It's a rare thing, what you've got there' has a genuine warmth that makes the preceding argument funnier by contrast.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: Dear Jemaine, by the time you read this, I will be far far away at Coco's house

jokes.text: Hi, I'm not disturbing you guys, am I? — Hey, Coco. — No, come in. — We're busy at the moment actually.
jokes.setup: Coco arrives at the band office/Murray's management office
jokes.punchline: Bret and Jemaine respond simultaneously — Bret with welcome, Jemaine with dismissal. The contrast reveals their opposing positions on Coco's presence in the band's world
jokes.explanation: A functional joke that characterizes the Bret/Jemaine conflict efficiently. The simultaneous yes/no response to Coco's arrival is clear and economical. Not especially inventive but serves the episode's structure.

dialogue.line: Lives are like retractable pencils / If you push 'em too hard, they're gonna break / And people are like paper dolls

jokes.text: Oh, okay. I just popped in to bring you some lunch. — Oh good. — Favoritism.
jokes.setup: Coco brings lunch for everyone in the office
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's one-word 'Favoritism' delivered as Coco hands out lunches — preemptively calling the distribution unfair even as it's happening, revealing his paranoia about Coco's presence
jokes.explanation: 'Favoritism.' — one word, delivered as if it's a legal objection. The joke works because Jemaine is clearly going to receive a lunch too (and does), so the accusation is premature and paranoid. The economy is exceptional — this single word communicates Jemaine's entire emotional position in the episode. Very quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: You search on the roll / You search on the roll / You're searchin' round the roll / You search with your fingernail / Again and again and again and again / And again

jokes.text: Coco presents a professional band poster she made. / Whoa, that's awesome. — Well, it's really arty. — It's cool. — We've already got a poster that I did. / Oh, okay. Oh, that's... that's all right. / That's cool. That's great, it's... it's better.
jokes.setup: Coco presents a band poster she made — apparently quite good
jokes.punchline: Murray's admission that Coco's poster is 'better' than the one he made — delivered with the deflation of a man who spent real effort on his poster — while Jemaine points out there's already a Murray-made poster as a Yoko-style territorial defense
jokes.explanation: The subtext is that Coco's artistic talent is exactly the Yoko-threat Murray predicted, arriving through a better poster. Murray's 'it's... it's better' admission is a nice small moment of honest defeat. The cringe is in the room's awkwardness after he says it.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Oh, I thought you... I was sure you were gonna say the band.

jokes.text: Also present, Coco. — Yoko. / What did you... what did you say? — Oh nothing, just had a bit of a cough.
jokes.setup: Murray adds Coco to the meeting minutes — Jemaine responds with 'Yoko' disguised as a cough
jokes.punchline: The 'cough' defense is entirely implausible — 'Yoko' doesn't sound like a cough — and Jemaine knows it
jokes.explanation: Classic passive-aggressive workplace move: the disguised insult delivered under plausible deniability. The deniability here is completely implausible ('Yoko' sounds nothing like a cough), which makes Jemaine's attempt funnier. The beat where everyone knows what was said but Jemaine maintains the fiction is good cringe structure.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: I guess you can still be in the band.

jokes.text: No, it wasn't a real cough. I said 'Yoko.' — Yeah, I thought so. — Oh, I should go. — Why did you say that? 'Cause she's nothing like Yoko. — Oh-no, she isn't. — Jemaine, you're putting 'oh no' together to sound like 'Ono.' — Oh-no, I didn't. — You did it again. — Oh-no, did I? — Did you pick that up? — He's doing it.
jokes.setup: Confronted about saying 'Yoko,' Jemaine begins defending himself — but keeps saying 'oh no' which sounds like 'Ono'
jokes.punchline: The more Jemaine tries to deny it, the more he says 'oh-no/Ono' — the denial is structurally identical to the accusation, trapping him in a loop. 'Oh-no, did I?' is the peak
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's best wordplay joke and one of its structural high points. The premise — that 'oh no' sounds like 'Ono' — is perfectly simple, but the execution is exceptional because every attempt to deny the accusation repeats the offense. 'Oh-no, I didn't. / You did it again. / Oh-no, did I?' The joke escalates with each exchange and the structure is self-reinforcing — the more Jemaine protests, the more he says it. Murray's 'Did you pick that up? He's doing it.' functions as the room's verification, making everyone complicit in witnessing the trap. This is above the 8.0 anchor because the structural elegance is genuine: the content of the denial is the crime.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Actually, I'm gonna wait till the bus stops, and then I'll get off.

jokes.text: I'll throw this sandwich in your face if you say that again. — Bret, don't you dare. Not in my office. / A sandwich hit my face. — Yeah, well, I said I'd throw a sandwich at your face.
jokes.setup: Bret threatens to throw a sandwich at Jemaine if he continues the Yoko references
jokes.punchline: He follows through — and Jemaine's flat 'A sandwich hit my face' is the most matter-of-fact description of being assaulted with food possible. Bret's justification 'I said I'd throw a sandwich at your face' treats the warning as contractual
jokes.explanation: 'A sandwich hit my face' is a wonderful deadpan damage report — the passive construction ('hit my face' rather than 'you threw a sandwich at my face') is either shock or a reflexive objectification of events. Bret's 'I said I'd throw a sandwich at your face' as justification is perfect — he made a clear commitment and honored it. Murray's 'Not in my office' as the primary objection to the sandwich-throwing is also good.

dialogue.speaker: Bret
dialogue.line: I've been thinking about love, and I guess it's the very strongest adhesive.

dialogue.speaker: Jemaine
dialogue.line: Oh, sorry, Bret, were you talking to me? I was humming.

jokes.text: I've never seen such bad behavior at one of my meetings! — Don't be such a... a dickhead. — Yeah well, I'm quitting this band. — Yeah. — Well, you quit last week. — Yeah well, I'm quitting again. — Don't be a quitter! You can't! — Yeah, you quitter.
jokes.setup: The meeting descends into chaos after the sandwich incident
jokes.punchline: The escalation from Murray's procedural outrage to 'dickhead' to quitting to 'you quit last week' reveals that quitting is a recurring pattern — and 'don't be a quitter' followed by 'yeah, you quitter' from Murray and Jemaine is the collapse of any moral authority in the room
jokes.explanation: 'Well, you quit last week' is the best moment here — the implication that quitting the band is such a regular occurrence that it's tracked. 'Yeah well, I'm quitting again' is the right response. Murray and Jemaine both calling Bret a quitter while Bret is the one being driven out is a nice absurdist inversion.

jokes.text: You've never been interested in bands anyway, Bret. You only came to one of the band rotunda tours.
jokes.setup: Murray is accusing Bret of being disloyal to the band
jokes.punchline: His evidence of Bret's lack of commitment is attending only ONE of the band rotunda tours — framing attendance at Murray's personal hobby as a measure of band loyalty
jokes.explanation: This is the episode's best callback — the band rotunda tour, established as a running joke in the opening, returns as Murray's evidence of Bret's disloyalty. The logic is perfectly Murray: the tours are a band obligation, attendance is proof of commitment, and one tour is insufficient. Character integration score of 9 because this could ONLY be Murray — no other character type would have this grievance. Strong craft.

jokes.text: It was a visa application denied. It took a long time to try and get resolved. I think the guy's a criminal. Sorry. / Just carry on. / It's looking good.
jokes.setup: The meeting has descended into chaos — quitting, sandwich-throwing, name-calling
jokes.punchline: Murray apparently explains to someone (presumably an uninvolved party present in the office) that the commotion was due to a visa application being denied — a completely inadequate explanation for what just happened — and then 'It's looking good' as reassurance
jokes.explanation: Murray's damage-control spin on a chaotic meeting scene — the visa application explanation is clearly inadequate for the observed chaos, and 'It's looking good' is an objectively false assessment. This suggests Murray runs his management office out of a shared space or has witnesses to the dysfunction. Mild but characterful.

jokes.text: Good riddance, eh, Jemaine? — I've got tomato on my sleeve. — See? I told you. / Girlfriends and bands just don't mix, Jemaine. — You'll never have a girlfriend, will you? — No. — All right, let's just move on.
jokes.setup: Bret has stormed out; Murray and Jemaine are alone
jokes.punchline: Murray consoles Jemaine with 'You'll never have a girlfriend, will you?' — delivered apparently as comfort rather than insult — and Jemaine's flat 'No' accepts this assessment with no apparent hurt
jokes.explanation: Murray's intended comfort — 'at least you'll never cause this problem because you'll never have a girlfriend' — is accidentally devastating, and Jemaine's quiet 'No' accepting it without protest is both funny and a little sad. The 'All right, let's just move on' pivot is Murray at his most managerial. 'I've got tomato on my sleeve' from Jemaine as his primary concern after the argument is also good.

jokes.text: Do you miss Bret? — A little bit. Do you? — Same. I miss him. / Can you ask him back in the band?
jokes.setup: After Bret quits, Murray and Jemaine immediately start missing him
jokes.punchline: Murray — the band manager, the authority figure — asks Jemaine if he can ask Bret back, as if this is Jemaine's responsibility. 'Can you ask him back in the band?' is a manager outsourcing his own management
jokes.explanation: The warmth of both admitting they miss Bret is undercut by Murray immediately delegating the reunion to Jemaine. The short time between the split and the missing ('all right, let's move on' / 'do you miss Bret?') suggests their resolve lasted about fifteen seconds. Nice character moment.

jokes.text: Dear Jemaine, by the time you read this, I will be far far away at Coco's house and I will no longer be residing... / and that is just one of the reasons why I'm moving out. You didn't seem to understand that... / And the most important thing for me to say is that... / Do you remember the time that seagull... / Yours sincerely, Bret.
jokes.setup: Jemaine goes to find Bret; we hear Bret's letter being read, apparently a formal declaration of moving out
jokes.punchline: The letter fragments reveal that Bret started multiple serious points and abandoned them all mid-sentence, apparently derailed by a seagull memory — then signs off formally as 'Yours sincerely, Bret' without completing a single thought
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's most original jokes. The letter is a series of incomplete declarations — 'I will no longer be residing...' / 'that is just one of the reasons...' / 'the most important thing... is that...' — each trailing off before the point is made, followed by what appears to be a random seagull anecdote, and then 'Yours sincerely, Bret.' The joke is that the formal letter-writing structure (Dear Jemaine, Yours sincerely) surrounds complete incoherence. The seagull is the gem — Bret was apparently distracted mid-grievance by a fond memory. The formal sign-off 'Yours sincerely' after all this is the perfect punctuation. Justifies 8.3: highly original structure, perfect character (this is exactly how Bret would handle emotional confrontation), and the economy of the ellipses does real work.

jokes.text: The 'Tape of Love' / 'Inner City Pressure' style song — full performance including 'Lives are like retractable pencils / If you push 'em too hard, they're gonna break / And people are like paper dolls / Paper dolls and people, they're a similar shape'
jokes.setup: The emotional low point of the episode — Bret has left, the band is broken; the scene calls for a sad, emotional musical moment
jokes.punchline: The song that emerges compares love to sellotape and lives to retractable pencils — the metaphors are simultaneously heartfelt and completely mundane/wrong. 'People are like paper dolls / paper dolls and people, they're a similar shape' is a philosophically hollow observation delivered with full sincerity
jokes.explanation: This is FOTC's characteristic mode at its best: a song that functions emotionally (we feel the melancholy of the band breaking up) while being completely absurd (the metaphor is stationery). 'Another way that love is similar to tape / That I've noticed / Is sometimes it's hard to see the end / You search on the roll / You search with your fingernail / Again and again and again' is brilliant — the mundane frustration of finding the end of a tape roll becomes a meditation on love's opacity. The specificity of 'fingernail' is the kind of detail that elevates the joke. 'Paper dolls and people, they're a similar shape' is a philosophical observation so thin it circles back to profound-sounding. The pencil callback to Murray's broken-pencil speech adds structural bonus. Justifies 8+ because the writing integrates genuine pathos with absurdist metaphor — a difficult tonal balance FOTC pulls off consistently.

jokes.text: 'Love is like a roll of tape / It's real good for making two things one / But just like that roll of tape / Love sometimes breaks off before you are done'
jokes.setup: The 'Tape of Love' song continues its series of metaphors.
jokes.punchline: The tape metaphor is developed with complete sincerity, culminating in 'love sometimes breaks off before you are done' — a genuinely mundane property of tape elevated to cosmic heartbreak status.
jokes.explanation: The tape-as-love metaphor works beautifully as comedy because it's almost plausible — tape does join things together — but the bathos of 'breaks off before you are done' applied to love is perfectly absurd. The song maintains absolute sincerity throughout, which is the essential element.

jokes.text: What are you doing here? — Murray and I miss you and we want you to rejoin the band again. / Really? — Yeah. On one condition. / That you... — I'm not gonna kill her, man.
jokes.setup: Jemaine arrives to ask Bret back to the band, beginning to state his condition
jokes.punchline: Before Jemaine finishes 'that you...', Bret's first pre-emptive response is 'I'm not gonna kill her, man' — implying that Bret's mental model of Jemaine's conditions involves murder as a possible option
jokes.explanation: One of the episode's best jokes. 'I'm not gonna kill her, man' is funny on multiple levels: (1) it reveals Bret thinks Jemaine is capable of demanding this, (2) it reveals Bret has apparently already considered and rejected this option before being asked, (3) the 'man' is the perfect casual register for refusing to commit murder. The economy is excellent — Jemaine doesn't even finish his sentence, and Bret's response tells us everything about how extreme their conflict has become while being completely absurd. Justifies 8.4+: highly quotable, character-specific (only Bret's dynamic with Jemaine makes this specific refusal land), and the misdirection structure is precise.

jokes.text: No, just leave... just leave her, not... — Oh no, I'm not gonna leave her.
jokes.setup: After Bret pre-emptively refuses murder, Jemaine clarifies the actual condition: just leave Coco
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's clarification ('not kill her, just leave her') treats 'leaving her' as the reasonable middle ground between murder and keeping her — the gap between the two options is enormous, but Jemaine presents them as a spectrum
jokes.explanation: Good extension of joke 59. Jemaine's correction of 'not kill her, just leave her' implicitly accepts that killing her was on the menu of options — he's just specifying a lesser demand. The structure requires joke 59 to land first, which it does.

jokes.text: I wouldn't mind, I mean if that's what you really want. — Shush, baby.
jokes.setup: Jemaine is pressuring Bret to choose: Coco or the band. Coco is apparently present for this ultimatum
jokes.punchline: Coco casually undermines her own position — 'I wouldn't mind, I mean if that's what you really want' — suggesting she's willing to be left, and Bret shushes her with 'Shush, baby' — the most domestically dismissive possible response
jokes.explanation: 'Shush, baby' — Bret dismissing his girlfriend's input during his romantic ultimatum is a lovely touch. Coco's willingness to step aside ('I don't really see us as long-term...') is also played for cringe, and Bret's multiple 'shush' silencings maintain his position. The dynamic inverts the romantic stakes — the person being fought over is the least invested.

jokes.text: So what's it gonna be, Bret? The girl or the band? — Well, it's gonna be Coco. — Oh. Are you sure? — Yeah. — Oh, I thought you... / I was sure you were gonna say the band. — No, I'm staying with Coco.
jokes.setup: Classic ultimatum structure — the girl or the band — Jemaine fully expects the band to win
jokes.punchline: Bret chooses Coco. Jemaine's 'Oh. Are you sure?' and 'I was sure you were gonna say the band' reveals his complete miscalculation — he issued the ultimatum believing the outcome was obvious
jokes.explanation: The classic ultimatum joke subverted: the person issuing the ultimatum assumed the outcome. Jemaine's 'I was sure you were gonna say the band' is a genuine character reveal — he thought the band was obviously more important than a girlfriend. The 'Are you sure?' suggests he's open to Bret reconsidering if he just thinks harder.

jokes.text: I guess you can still be in the band. — Okay. Great. — Okay. — Cool. — Okay, well I'll see you later. — I'll see you at band practice.
jokes.setup: Bret has chosen Coco over the band — Jemaine has presumably suffered a defeat
jokes.punchline: Jemaine's 'I guess you can still be in the band' — granted as a concession, as if the ultimatum still carries power and Jemaine is being generous by readmitting Bret despite Bret choosing wrong. The casualness of 'I guess' is perfect
jokes.explanation: 'I guess you can still be in the band' — Jemaine has completely lost the ultimatum but delivers the resolution as if he's magnanimously allowing Bret back. The 'I guess' does all the work: it implies he has the authority to exclude Bret (he doesn't, or it's at least mutual) and that he's settling for an imperfect outcome. Character integration: this is pure Jemaine — saving face by reframing defeat as generosity. Justifies 8+: the economy is exceptional (six words restructure the power dynamic), and the character specificity is exact.

jokes.text: Actually, I'm gonna wait till the bus stops, and then I'll get off.
jokes.setup: Bret and Jemaine have concluded their reconciliation; presumably one of them is on a bus that's still moving
jokes.punchline: Bret announces he'll wait for the bus to stop before getting off — stated as if this is a noteworthy decision requiring announcement, implying he was considering getting off a moving bus
jokes.explanation: Classic FOTC non-sequitur deadpan: Bret's statement implies he was considering dismounting a moving bus, but has now decided against it. The mundanity of the decision — described as a careful choice — is the joke. 'Actually' suggests deliberation preceded this conclusion. Very quotable as a standalone non-sequitur. The economy is perfect — one sentence with an implied insane alternative.

jokes.text: You know, Jemaine, I've been thinking about love, / and I guess it's the very strongest adhesive. / Oh, sorry, Bret, were you talking to me? I was humming. / What did you say? — Oh, doesn't matter.
jokes.setup: Bret makes a philosophical observation about love as the song winds down — a sincere emotional beat
jokes.punchline: Jemaine has been humming and wasn't listening — Bret's emotional observation about love goes completely unheard. 'What did you say? — Oh, doesn't matter' — Bret gives up rather than repeat himself
jokes.explanation: A gentle closing joke: Bret's sincere emotional observation ('love is the very strongest adhesive' — a callback to the tape song's metaphor) is delivered into Jemaine's inattention. The fact that Bret says 'oh, doesn't matter' rather than repeating himself suggests he half-knows this is both sincere and slightly ridiculous. The warmth of the friendship is preserved despite the inattention. Nice closer.

tmdb_overview: Bret's relationship with Coco threatens to split up the duo.
